 {"id":"bd-1iwi","title":"Workstream: Interactive Editor UX Parity (autocomplete, multiline, bang, images)","description":"# Goal
Bring the **interactive editor experience** to parity with legacy pi-mono, focusing on:
- Autocomplete (commands, templates, skills, file references, path completion)
- Multi-line editing behavior (Shift+Enter inserts newline, Enter submits)
- Bash shortcuts (`!` and `!!`)
- Clipboard image paste + drag/drop attachments

# Legacy Behavior (Source-of-Truth)
From `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/README.md` → “Editor”:
- File reference: type `@` to fuzzy-search project files
- Path completion: Tab to complete paths
- Multi-line: Shift+Enter (Ctrl+Enter on Windows Terminal)
- Images: Ctrl+V to paste, or drag onto terminal
- Bash commands: `!command` runs and sends output to LLM, `!!command` runs without sending

Legacy implementation details:
- Autocomplete is provided by `CombinedAutocompleteProvider` (slash commands, prompt templates, extension commands, skill commands, file path completion using `fd`).
- Image paste writes clipboard image to a temp file and inserts the file path into the editor.

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `src/interactive.rs` reserves Tab for future autocomplete but has no autocomplete UI.
- No `@` file fuzzy search.
- No Shift+Enter newline semantics (current behavior differs).
- No `!` / `!!` bash shortcuts.
- No clipboard image paste handler.

# Scope / Deliverables
## 1) Autocomplete engine
- Provide suggestions for:
  - Slash commands (built-in)
  - Prompt templates (`/<template>`)
  - Skill commands (`/skill:name`) when enabled
  - File references after typing `@` (fuzzy search project files)
  - Path completion when cursor is inside a path token

## 2) Autocomplete UI
- Render a dropdown/list with:
  - configurable max visible items (`autocomplete_max_visible`)
  - keyboard navigation
  - selection/accept behavior

## 3) Multi-line input semantics
- Shift+Enter inserts newline; Enter submits.
- Windows Terminal: Ctrl+Enter inserts newline.

## 4) Bash shortcuts
- `!cmd`: run and include output in LLM context.
- `!!cmd`: run but do not include output in LLM context.

## 5) Clipboard image paste and drag/drop
- Ctrl+V attempts to read clipboard image:
  - if an image exists, write it to a temp file and insert the file path at cursor.
  - do nothing (silent) if clipboard has no image or access denied.
- Drag/drop: terminals usually paste a file path; ensure we handle file paths cleanly.

# Testing
- Unit tests for autocomplete matching and insertion.
- State tests for key interactions (Tab, Shift+Enter, Ctrl+V).
- Integration tests for `!` / `!!` behavior (using deterministic command runner where possible).

# Dependencies
- Keybinding routing (`bd-3ip`) for Tab / Shift+Enter / Ctrl+V behavior.

## Acceptance Criteria
[ ] Autocomplete exists (commands/templates/skills/files/paths)
[ ] Tab path completion works
[ ] @ fuzzy file reference works
[ ] Shift+Enter inserts newline; Enter submits
[ ] ! and !! bash shortcuts implemented
[ ] Ctrl+V image paste + drag/drop attachment supported
[ ] Editor settings are wired (padding, max visible)

 {"id":"bd-1jn","title":"Impl: Runtime hardening (limits, watchdog, accounting)","description":"# Goal
Harden the PiJS runtime so extension execution is **bounded, observable, and safe**.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Memory cap + allocation tracking for QuickJS runtime.
- CPU/time budget via interrupt hook or watchdog.
- Hostcall timeouts enforced centrally.
- Structured metrics: per-extension CPU, memory, hostcall counts.
- Explicit termination reasons with user-friendly messages.

# Security / Reliability Invariants
- Extension cannot exceed configured budgets.
- Violations terminate the extension in a controlled, logged way.

# Tests
- Unit tests for budget exceed -> deterministic error.
- E2E security suite validates enforcement + log output.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:22:22.240908810Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:06:55.297533242Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1jn","depends_on_id":"bd-2ke","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T17:24:49.136502941Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1kza","title":"Interactive: /share uploads session HTML as private GitHub gist","description":"# Goal
Implement gist-backed `/share` parity with legacy.

# Required Behavior
1) Verify `gh` exists.
2) Verify `gh auth status` succeeds.
3) Export session to a temp HTML file.
4) Show a cancellable loader while running:
   - `gh gist create --public=false <tmpFile>`

   - parse the returned gist URL
   - extract gist ID
   - compute viewer URL (see `bd-2uji`)
   - show both viewer URL and gist URL
6) Cleanup temp file.

- If user cancels while `gh` is running:
  - kill the process
  - restore the editor
  - show status “Share cancelled”

 {"id":"bd-1mbs","title":"Editor: Ctrl+V image paste + drag/drop attachment","description":"# Goal
Support image attachment UX in interactive mode:
- Ctrl+V to paste an image from clipboard.
- Drag/drop a file onto the terminal to attach.

# Legacy Behavior
- On Ctrl+V, legacy tries to read clipboard image; if present, it writes to a temp file and inserts the file path at cursor.
  - See `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/src/modes/interactive/interactive-mode.ts` (`handleClipboardImagePaste`).

# Required Behavior
- Ctrl+V:
  - If clipboard contains an image, write it to a temp file (png/jpg based on mime if possible).
  - Insert the resulting path into the editor at the cursor.
  - If clipboard has no image or access denied, do nothing silently.

- Drag/drop:
  - Many terminals paste a quoted or escaped file path. Ensure we normalize/sanitize so that subsequent file reference processing can resolve it.

- [ ] Users can attach an image without manually saving a file.
- [ ] No crashes when clipboard access is unavailable.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:05.385818509Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:42.357212214Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1mbs","depends_on_id":"bd-1iwi","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:05.395051624Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1mbs","depends_on_id":"bd-gze","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:42.357181036Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-1ry","title":"Impl: WebAssembly bridge for PiJS (QuickJS has no wasm)","description":"# Assumption / Constraint
Assume **QuickJS does not provide WebAssembly** (`globalThis.WebAssembly` absent). We still must run the full pinned sample set, including extensions that ship Emscripten-style JS+WASM bundles.

# Goal
Provide a **PiWasm bridge** so JS-tier extensions can use a **WebAssembly-compatible API** even though the JS engine lacks wasm.

# Genericity constraint (non-negotiable)
- **No per-extension exceptions**: the bridge must not branch on extension id/name.
- Behavior is determined only by: wasm bytes, import object shape, policy/capabilities, and deterministic runtime configuration.
- Sample set is only used to define the **minimum required semantics**, not to justify one-off hacks.

# Strategy (hybrid)
- Use **wasmtime** (same engine as Tier A WASM host) to execute wasm.
- Expose a minimal `globalThis.WebAssembly` polyfill (or equivalent injected module) that is **generically compatible** with common JS+WASM glue patterns.
- All imports that represent side effects must route through **connector hostcalls** and therefore capabilities/logging.

# Scope / Deliverables
## WebAssembly JS surface (subset)
- `WebAssembly.instantiate(bytes, imports)`
- `WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming(resp, imports)` (if needed; otherwise extc rewrite to non-streaming)
- `WebAssembly.compile(bytes)` / `WebAssembly.Module`
- `WebAssembly.Instance` exports behavior used by typical glue code
- `WebAssembly.Memory` with `.buffer` semantics compatible with standard glue patterns
- Traps/errors mapped to actionable Pi errors + structured logs

## Import object handling
- Support JS functions as wasm imports with deterministic call ordering.
- Restrict / gate imports that imply OS access.

## Caching + perf
- Cache compiled wasm modules keyed by sha256(bytes + wasmtime_version + policy_version).
- Minimize cross-boundary copies; measure and optimize boundary overhead.

# Security constraints
- Enforce memory/table limits per extension policy.
- Ensure wasm cannot access host OS except via explicit connector imports.
- Structured logs: every instantiate/compile includes extension_id + artifact_id + elapsed + limits.

# Acceptance (hard)
- Any **wasm-using JS-tier artifact** in the pinned sample set runs end-to-end **without manual source edits** using this bridge (or Tier A when appropriate).
- The E2E harness passes **16/16** sample extensions.
- The implementation remains **generic** (no extension-id special-casing), enforced by tests/review.

# Tests (required)
- Unit tests: wasm compile/instantiate, memory growth boundaries, import call routing, trap mapping.
- Conformance fixtures for wasm-in-js scenarios with deterministic traces.
- Security tests: denied connector imports, exceeded limits, malicious wasm.

# Dependencies
- Must compose with unified capability model (bd-2hr) and dispatcher enforcement (bd-h04).","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:06.245104290Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T18:59:24.479415261Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-123","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:55.694562907Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-1f5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:53.637067219Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-1jn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:55.154828574Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-2hr","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:52.626419314Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-2ki","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:54.137059371Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-37z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:54.645059374Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-3d1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:52.093479872Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1ry","depends_on_id":"bd-h04","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T18:37:53.128787815Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3ml","title":"Implement verbose test logging infrastructure","description":"# Implement verbose test logging infrastructure

## Goal
Create shared infrastructure for detailed logging in all integration tests,
enabling easy debugging when tests fail.

## Background
E2E and integration tests need detailed logging to debug failures:
- Every test action should be logged
- Timestamps for timing analysis
- Context (file paths, values) for reproduction
- Logs dumped automatically on test failure

## Implementation

### Log Infrastructure
```rust
// tests/common/logging.rs

use std::sync::Mutex;
use std::time::Instant;

#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct LogEntry {
    pub timestamp: Instant,
    pub level: LogLevel,
    pub category: &'static str,
    pub message: String,
    pub context: Vec<(String, String)>,
}

#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
pub enum LogLevel {
    Debug,
    Info,
    Warn,
    Error,
}

pub struct TestLogger {
    entries: Mutex<Vec<LogEntry>>,
    start: Instant,
}
```

### Redaction + Artifacts
- Add helpers to redact sensitive values in env/header/context keys (API keys, auth headers).
- Add a simple artifact registry: `record_artifact(name, path)` so tests can attach outputs.
- On panic, dump logs + artifact list (paths) to stderr.

### Test Harness Integration
```rust
// tests/common/harness.rs

pub struct TestHarness {
    pub logger: TestLogger,
    pub temp_dir: TempDir,
}

impl Drop for TestHarness {
    fn drop(&mut self) {
        if std::thread::panicking() {
            eprintln!("\n=== TEST LOGS ===\n{}", self.logger.dump());
            eprintln!("=== ARTIFACTS ===\n{}", self.logger.dump_artifacts());
        }
    }
}
```

### Optional File Output
- If `TEST_LOG_PATH` is set, write logs to that path in addition to stderr.

## Files
- tests/common/mod.rs
- tests/common/logging.rs
- tests/common/harness.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TestLogger captures all levels
- [ ] Context key-values supported
- [ ] Timestamps included
- [ ] Redaction helpers present
- [ ] Artifact registry present
- [ ] Auto-dump on test failure
- [ ] Optional file output works
- [ ] All integration tests use harness","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:40:54.070756084Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T05:48:33.124493922Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T05:48:33.124430233Z","close_reason":"Implemented redaction helpers, artifact registry + dump, and TEST_LOG_PATH file output in test logging; harness now dumps artifacts on panic and provides record_artifact.  {"id":"bd-3nz","title":"Wire session indexing into session.persist()","description":"# Wire session indexing into session.persist()

## Goal
Automatically update the SQLite index when sessions are saved.

## Implementation

### Add Index Update to persist()
```rust
// src/session.rs

impl Session {
    pub async fn persist(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<()> {
        // Existing JSONL write logic
        self.write_jsonl(path).await?;
        
        // NEW: Update index
        if let Ok(index) = SessionIndex::open_default() {
            if let Err(e) = index.index_session(path) {
                // Log warning but don't fail persist
                tracing::warn!("Failed to update session index: {}", e);
            }
        }
        
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Default Index Path
```rust
// src/session_index.rs

impl SessionIndex {
    pub fn open_default() -> Result<Self> {
        let path = dirs::data_dir()
            .unwrap_or_else(|| PathBuf::from("."))
            .join(".pi/agent/sessions/index.db");
        Self::open(&path)
    }
}
```

### Handle Index Creation
- Create index.db if not exists
- Use WAL mode for concurrent access
- Handle permission errors gracefully

## Considerations
1. **Performance**: Index update should be fast (<10ms)
2. **Reliability**: Don't fail persist if index fails
3. **Concurrency**: Handle multiple pi instances
4. **Migration**: Handle existing sessions without index entries

## Testing
- Test: persist() updates index
- Test: persist() succeeds even if index fails
- Test: Index contains session after persist

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] persist() calls index_session()
- [ ] Index created if not exists
- [ ] Errors logged but don't fail persist
- [ ] Performance impact <10ms","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:32.093344392Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:22:26.066864853Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T17:22:06.998331380Z","close_reason":"Completed: Session::save() now updates SessionIndex (best-effort); index stored under sessions root; redundant index_session calls removed; test added.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3nz","depends_on_id":"bd-346","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:38:32.099125014Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":24,"issue_id":"bd-3nz","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Picking up bd-3nz.  {"id":"bd-9sa","title":"Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper","description":"# Implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper

## Goal
Create a functional HTTP client in src/http/client.rs using asupersync APIs.

## Current State
src/http/client.rs has:
- Client struct (empty)
- RequestBuilder struct with method/url/headers/body
- RequestBuilder::send() returns Err("asupersync HTTP client not yet implemented")
- RequestBuilder::json() silently ignores serialization errors

## Implementation Requirements

### Client API
```rust
use asupersync::{Cx, http::client::HttpClient};
use asupersync::tls::TlsConnectorBuilder;

pub struct Client {
    inner: HttpClient,
}

impl Client {
    pub fn new() -> Result<Self, Error> {
        // Create TLS connector with native roots
        let tls = TlsConnectorBuilder::new()
            .with_native_roots()?
            .build()?;
        let inner = HttpClient::new(tls);
        Ok(Self { inner })
    }
    
    pub fn request(&self, method: Method, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder {
        RequestBuilder::new(self, method, url)
    }
    
    pub fn get(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
    pub fn post(&self, url: &str) -> RequestBuilder { ... }
}
```

### RequestBuilder API
```rust
impl RequestBuilder {
    pub fn header(mut self, key: &str, value: &str) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn json<T: Serialize>(mut self, body: &T) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn body(mut self, bytes: Vec<u8>) -> Self { ... }
    pub fn timeout(mut self, duration: Duration) -> Self { ... }
    
    pub async fn send(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<Response, Error> {
        // Actually send the request using asupersync
    }
}
```

### Response API
```rust
pub struct Response {
    status: StatusCode,
    headers: Headers,
    body: BodyReader,  // Async streaming body
}

impl Response {
    pub fn status(&self) -> StatusCode { ... }
    pub fn headers(&self) -> &Headers { ... }
    pub async fn text(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<String, Error> { ... }
    pub async fn json<T: DeserializeOwned>(self, cx: &Cx) -> Result<T, Error> { ... }
    pub fn bytes_stream(self) -> impl Stream<Item = Result<Bytes, Error>> { ... }
}
```

## Key Considerations
1. **TLS**: Use rustls with native root certificates (no openssl)
2. **Timeout**: Support per-request timeout via asupersync Budget
3. **Streaming**: Response body must support async streaming for SSE
4. **Headers**: Support standard headers (Content-Type, Authorization, etc.)
5. **Error handling**: Wrap asupersync errors in pi's Error type

## Testing
- Unit test: Client creation succeeds
- Unit test: RequestBuilder builds correct request
- Integration test: GET request to httpbin.org/get
- Integration test: POST with JSON body
- Integration test: Timeout behavior

## Files
- src/http/client.rs (modify)
- src/http/mod.rs (update re-exports)
- src/error.rs (add HTTP error variants)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Client::new() creates working HTTP client
- [ ] GET/POST requests work
- [ ] JSON body serialization works
- [ ] Response streaming works
- [ ] Timeout respected
- [ ] TLS verification works
- [ ] All unit tests pass","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:01.120130883Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:08.576779013Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T17:12:08.576706438Z","close_reason":"Completed (HTTP client wrapper is implemented + tests pass; see comments)","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-9sa","depends_on_id":"bd-gi3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T03:32:01.127860687Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":1,"issue_id":"bd-9sa","author":"AmberForest","text":"Taking this bead: implement asupersync HTTP client wrapper (no tokio/reqwest).  {"id":"bd-ah1","title":"Implement session persistence cycle tests","description":"# Implement session persistence cycle tests

## Goal
Test the full session lifecycle: create → save → reload → verify integrity, with detailed logs.

## Background
Session persistence involves:
- JSONL file format (one JSON object per line)
- Header with metadata (version, cwd, timestamp)
- Message entries with parent/child relationships
- Tree structure for conversation branching
- File locking for concurrent access

Current tests cover serialization but not full I/O cycles.

## Runtime + Determinism
- Do not introduce new tokio-only tests; use `#[asupersync::test]` or a runtime wrapper.
- Use temp dirs and deterministic paths; avoid asserting exact timestamps.

## Logging Requirements
- Use TestLogger (bd-3ml).
- Log session path, header fields, message IDs, tree shape, and file sizes.
- On failure, dump the JSONL contents (redacted) and parsed header.

## Test Categories

### 1. Basic Persistence Cycle Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_session_save_and_reload() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project/path");
    session.add_user_message("Hello");
    session.add_assistant_message("Hi there!");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.messages().len(), 2);
}
```

### 2. Tree Structure Persistence Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_branching_persists() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/project");
    let root_id = session.add_user_message("Root");
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch A");

    session.navigate_to(root_id.clone());
    session.add_assistant_message("Branch B");

    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    let children = loaded.children_of(&root_id);
    assert_eq!(children.len(), 2);
}
```

### 3. Header Metadata Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_header_metadata_preserved() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let mut session = Session::new("/my/project/path");
    session.set_provider("anthropic");
    session.set_model("claude-opus-4");
    session.set_thinking_level(ThinkingLevel::High);
    session.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let loaded = Session::load(&session_path).await.unwrap();
    assert_eq!(loaded.cwd(), "/my/project/path");
}
```

### 4. Entry Type Persistence Tests
- ModelChange entries
- ThinkingLevel entries
- Compaction entries
- Tool invocation entries (if serialized)

### 5. Error Handling Tests
- Missing file
- Corrupted JSONL
- Partial corruption (valid header + bad line)

### 6. Concurrent Access Tests
```rust
#[asupersync::test]
async fn test_concurrent_writes_locked() {
    let temp = TempDir::new().unwrap();
    let session_path = temp.path().join("test.jsonl");

    let session1 = Session::new("/project");
    session1.persist(&session_path).await.unwrap();

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle1 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 1");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let path = session_path.clone();
    let handle2 = spawn(async move {
        let mut s = Session::load(&path).await.unwrap();
        s.add_user_message("From task 2");
        s.persist(&path).await
    });

    let (r1, r2) = join(handle1, handle2);
    assert!(r1.unwrap().is_ok() || r2.unwrap().is_ok());
}
```

### 7. Directory Structure Tests
- Persist creates nested directories when needed

## Dependencies
None (uses existing session module)

## Files
- tests/session_persistence.rs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 15+ session persistence tests
- [ ] Save/reload cycle tested
- [ ] Tree structure preserved
- [ ] All entry types tested
- [ ] Error handling tested
- [ ] Concurrent access tested
- [ ] Logs include paths + header fields

-{"id":"bd-axuu","title":"Workstream: /settings UI Parity + config persistence","description":"# Goal
Bring `/settings` and config editing UX to parity with legacy pi-mono:
- `/settings` opens an in-app UI for common options.
- Changes persist to JSON settings files with correct precedence (global vs project).

# Legacy Spec
- Settings docs: `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/docs/settings.md`
- `/settings` is listed in interactive command table in legacy README.

Legacy `/settings` covers (at least):
- thinking level
- theme
- message delivery (steering/follow-up)
- model cycling scope

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `/settings` prints a JSON summary only; no editor UI.
- Several config fields exist in `src/config.rs` but are not wired into the interactive UX.

# Scope / Deliverables
1) Implement a `/settings` UI component:
   - select/toggle settings
   - show current value
   - confirm/apply
2) Persist changes:
   - write to `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` (global) and/or `.pi/settings.json` (project)
   - follow existing merge semantics from `src/config.rs`
3) Ensure changes take effect without restart where possible.

# Notes
- Avoid editing raw JSON in the TUI for now; prefer focused selectors.
- For settings that depend on other workstreams (themes, keybindings), include partial functionality but keep UX consistent.

# Testing
- Unit tests for persistence and merge semantics.
- State tests for `/settings` selection flows.

+{"id":"bd-axuu","title":"Workstream: /settings UI Parity + config persistence","description":"# Goal
Bring `/settings` and config editing UX to parity with legacy pi-mono:
- `/settings` opens an in-app UI for common options.
- Changes persist to JSON settings files with correct precedence (global vs project).

# Legacy Spec
- Settings docs: `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/docs/settings.md`
- `/settings` is listed in interactive command table in legacy README.

Legacy `/settings` covers (at least):
- thinking level
- theme
- message delivery (steering/follow-up)
- model cycling scope

# Current Rust State (Gap)
- `/settings` prints a JSON summary only; no editor UI.
- Several config fields exist in `src/config.rs` but are not wired into the interactive UX.

# Scope / Deliverables
1) Implement a `/settings` UI component:
   - select/toggle settings
   - show current value
   - confirm/apply
2) Persist changes:
   - write to `~/.pi/agent/settings.json` (global) and/or `.pi/settings.json` (project)
   - follow existing merge semantics from `src/config.rs`
3) Ensure changes take effect without restart where possible.

# Notes
- Avoid editing raw JSON in the TUI for now; prefer focused selectors.
- For settings that depend on other workstreams (themes, keybindings), include partial functionality but keep UX consistent.

# Testing
- Unit tests for persistence and merge semantics.
- State tests for `/settings` selection flows.

# Dependencies
- Keybinding workstream (`bd-3ip`) for consistent navigation.
- Theme system workstream (`bd-22p`) for actual theme switching.

## Acceptance Criteria
[ ] /settings opens interactive UI and restores editor
[ ] Changes persist to global/project settings
[ ] Message delivery settings configurable
[ ] Theme selection supported (once theme system exists)
[ ] Display/editor toggles configurable

 {"id":"bd-btq","title":"Extensions: Node/Bun-free runtime (PiJS) — prove superior path","description":"# Goal
Deliver a Node/Bun-free extension runtime (PiJS) that is **more secure, more deterministic, and faster** than legacy Node/Bun extensions, while ensuring **all 16 extensions in `docs/extension-sample.json` run unmodified**.

- “Unmodified” = no manual edits to extension source. Normal install/build steps and the **extc compile/rewrite pipeline** are allowed as part of the runtime.

# Background / Motivation
- Mario’s critique is correct: QuickJS lacks Node/Bun OS wrappers and event loop. We replace that with a **minimal, capability-gated PiJS runtime + deterministic event loop**.
- The win condition is not “be Node”; the win condition is: **run the real extensions**, with a smaller surface, better security posture, and better observability.

# Genericity constraint (non-negotiable)
- **No per-extension exceptions** in runtime, shims, or compiler rewrites.
- All behavior differences must be attributable to: artifact bytes, manifest/capabilities, policy mode, and deterministic runtime config.

# Strategy (PiJS)
1) **Connector-first architecture** — all I/O is explicit `host_call` into the connector dispatcher; no ambient OS APIs.
2) **Deterministic event loop** — microtasks, timers, hostcall promises driven by asupersync with strict ordering rules.
3) **Hybrid WASM approach (assume QuickJS has no WebAssembly)**
   - Tier A: WASM components via the WASM host (bd-3d1/bd-nom).
   - Tier B: JS compatibility via QuickJS **bytecode**.
   - For JS-tier bundles that expect `globalThis.WebAssembly`: provide a **PiWasm bridge** backed by wasmtime (bd-1ry).
4) **Node-compat shims (surgical, sample-driven)**
   - Implement only the Node core APIs actually required by the pinned sample set.
   - Provide `node:*` shims and globals (`process`, `Buffer`, etc.) implemented on top of connectors with capability enforcement (bd-3d0, bd-2sr).
5) **Compatibility tooling** — extc rewrite + compat scanner. Unsupported APIs rejected with actionable diagnostics and evidence ledgers.
6) **Safety + performance proof** — explicit capabilities, resource limits, benchmarks with reproducible outputs.
7) **Developer UX** — structured logs + trace viewer + remediation hints.
8) **Testing** — comprehensive unit, conformance, and E2E scripts with detailed logging artifacts.

# Success Criteria (hard)
- **16/16 pinned sample extensions** run end-to-end with **no manual source edits**.
- Deterministic ordering across repeated deterministic runs (trace equality where expected).
- Capability policy enforced for every hostcall with structured logs.
- Benchmarks meet or beat budgets, and regressions are catchable.
- Evidence binder (docs + logs + benchmarks) is reproducible.

# Non-Goals
- Full Node/Bun parity beyond what is required for the pinned sample set.
- Implicit global OS access.

# Notes for Future Self
This is the umbrella for the **"prove them wrong"** path. Any new capability surface must be: capability-gated, deterministic under test, and covered by unit + E2E tests with structured  {"id":"bd-cru","title":"Keybindings: define action catalog + default bindings","description":"# Goal
Create the authoritative list of interactive **actions** and their **default keybindings**, matching legacy Pi Agent.

# Background
Legacy action list and defaults are documented in:
- `legacy_pi_mono_code/pi-mono/packages/coding-agent/docs/keybindings.md`

This task is the foundation for parsing config overrides and routing key events.

# Scope / Deliverables
- Define an `AppAction`-like enum (or equivalent) with all actions from the legacy docs:
  - Cursor movement
  - Deletion
  - Text input (`newLine`, `submit`, `tab`)
  - Kill ring (`yank`, `yankPop`, `undo`) — if not implemented yet, still define actions for future parity
  - Clipboard (`copy`, `pasteImage`)
  - Application (`interrupt`, `clear`, `exit`, `suspend`, `externalEditor`)
  - Session (`newSession`, `tree`, `fork`)
  - Models & thinking (`selectModel`, `cycleModelForward/backward`, `cycleThinkingLevel`)
  - Display (`expandTools`, `toggleThinking`)
  - Message queue (`followUp`, `dequeue`)
  - Selection lists/pickers
  - Session picker actions

- Define the default bindings for each action (keys list) matching the legacy doc.

# Implementation Notes
- Keep the action IDs stable (strings or serde names) so they round-trip with JSON config.
- Prefer a single source of truth for:
  - action id (machine)
  - display name (human)
  - default keys
  - category (for `/hotkeys` grouping)

# Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Action list matches legacy (no missing actions).
- [ ] Default bindings match legacy defaults.
- [ ] There is a way to iterate actions (for `/hotkeys` rendering).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-03T19:36:08.298061995Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:08.671004588Z","closed_at":"2026-02-03T19:40:08.670942141Z","close_reason":"Created keybindings.rs with full action catalog (48 actions in 12 categories), default bindings matching legacy, JSON serialization support.
